question: According to the lecture slide shown early in the lecture, what is the full name of the acronym SRTBOT, and how does this connect to the memoization idea introduced later when solving the Fibonacci problem?
answer: SRTBOT stands for Subproblems, Relate subproblem solutions recursively, Topological order on subproblems to guarantee acyclic subproblem call DAG, Base cases of relation, Original problem solve via subproblems, and Time analysis. Dynamic programming builds on this template by adding memoization, which means remembering and reusing solutions to subproblems by maintaining a dictionary mapping subproblems to their solutions, allowing the exponential naive Fibonacci recursion to become polynomial time by solving each subproblem only once.

ground_truth_spans.description: Frame caption showing SRTBOT stands for Subproblems, Relate, Topological order, Base cases, Original problem, Time analysis
Introduction of memoization as big idea: remember and reuse solutions to subproblems by maintaining dictionary mapping subproblems to solutions

question: The lecture uses merge sort as an example of the SRTBOT paradigm. Based on the board shown for merge sort, what is the subproblem definition and topological order, and how does the running time analysis using the memoization formula compare to the standard merge sort analysis?
answer: The subproblem S(i,j) is defined as the sorted array on A[i:j], and the topological order is increasing j minus i, meaning shorter subarrays are solved before longer ones. When analyzing merge sort using the memoization formula, summing the non-recursive work over all theta n-squared subproblems, each costing theta(j-i) time, yields an upper bound of O(n cubed), which is weaker than the standard O(n log n) result. The lecture notes that this memoization formula gives a polynomial upper bound but is not tight for merge sort, which already runs in theta(n log n) time.

ground_truth_spans.description: Frame caption showing merge sort subproblems S(i,j)=sorted array on A[i:j], topo order increasing j-i, time T(n)=2T(n/2)+O(n)=O(n log n)
Analysis showing theta n-squared subproblems, time per subproblem theta(j-i), sum gives n cubed upper bound versus actual theta(n log n)

question: Based on the board captions showing the Fibonacci recurrence and the explanation of memoization, why does the naive recursive Fibonacci algorithm run in exponential time, and what exactly does memoization change to make it polynomial?
answer: The naive recursive Fibonacci algorithm has running time satisfying T(n) = T(n-1) + T(n-2) + 1, which is the same recurrence as the Fibonacci numbers themselves. Since Fibonacci numbers grow exponentially as approximately phi to the n (the golden ratio), the algorithm requires exponentially many additions. Memoization changes this by maintaining a dictionary mapping each subproblem F(i) to its solution; when a subproblem is needed, the algorithm first checks the memo table and returns the stored solution if available rather than recomputing it. Since each of the n subproblems is solved at most once with constant non-recursive work each, the total is only O(n) additions.

ground_truth_spans.description: Explanation that T(n)=T(n-1)+T(n-2)+1 gives exponential time bigger than phi^n, exponential BAD
Memoization analysis: n subproblems each solved once with constant work gives O(n) total time

question: The lecture introduces the subproblem design trick for sequence inputs and then applies it to the bowling problem. What are the three types of good subproblems listed on the board for sequence inputs, and which type does the bowling DP use and why?
answer: The board shows that when the input is a sequence X of length n, three types of good subproblems are prefixes X[:i] giving theta(n) subproblems, suffixes X[i:] giving theta(n) subproblems, and substrings X[i:j] giving theta(n squared) subproblems. The bowling DP uses suffixes, defining B(i) as the maximum score possible starting with pins i through n-1. Suffixes are chosen because when processing pin i, skipping it or hitting it either alone or paired with pin i+1 always leaves a smaller suffix as the remaining subproblem, so the recurrence naturally reduces to suffix subproblems of larger starting index.

ground_truth_spans.description: Frame caption showing subproblem design trick: prefixes X[:i], suffixes X[i:], substrings X[i:j] with their counts
Bowling DP uses suffixes: B(i) = max score possible starting with pins i, i+1, ..., n-1

question: The lecture shows a board for DAG shortest paths in the SRTBOT framework and also mentions that a generic DP algorithm implicitly performs DFS. How do the subproblem dependencies in DAG shortest paths relate to the original graph, and what does this reveal about the connection between dynamic programming and depth-first search?
answer: In the DAG shortest paths DP, the subproblem S(s,v) representing the shortest path from source s to vertex v depends on S(s,u) for all vertices u with an incoming edge to v; these dependencies exactly mirror the incoming edges of the original graph G. The topological order of the subproblem graph is therefore the topological order of G itself. A generic DP algorithm that checks a memo table before recursing is implicitly performing a depth-first search through the reverse of the subproblem dependency graph, with the memo table serving as the visited-vertex check. Thus dynamic programming subsumes depth-first search, and the finishing order of the DFS corresponds to computing subproblems in the correct topological order.

ground_truth_spans.description: Frame caption showing DAG shortest paths: S(s,v)=min{S(s,u)+w(u,v)|u in Adj(v)}, topo order = topological order of G
Generic DP algorithm is implicitly a DFS through the subproblem graph with memo table as visited check

question: The lecture explains that the bowling problem's recurrence has three cases. What are the three cases shown on the board for the recurrence relation B(i), and how does the final bottom-up implementation encode these cases as code?
answer: The three cases for B(i) shown on the board are: first, skip pin i and score B(i+1); second, hit pin i alone and score B(i+1) plus v_i; third, hit pins i and i+1 together as a pair and score B(i+2) plus v_i times v_{i+1}, which is only valid when i is strictly less than n minus 1. The bottom-up implementation initializes B(n) = 0 as the base case, then runs a for loop from i equals n down to 0, computing B(i) as the maximum of these three options at each step, with a conditional check that the third option is only included if i is less than n minus 1, and finally returns B(0).

ground_truth_spans.description: Frame caption showing B(i)=max(B(i+1), B(i+1)+Vi, B(i+2)+Vi*Vi+1) as the three-case recurrence for bowling DP
Bottom-up implementation: B(n)=0, for i=n,n-1,...,0: B(i)=max{...}, return B(0)

question: The lecture discusses why subsequences are excluded as a subproblem type for sequence inputs and also explains why dynamic programming achieves polynomial time despite exploring many options. How do these two ideas together explain the power and the limits of the subproblem design approach?
answer: Subsequences are excluded because for a sequence of length n there are 2 to the n subsequences—exponentially many—which would guarantee an exponential number of subproblems and defeat the purpose. In contrast, prefixes and suffixes give only theta(n) subproblems and substrings give theta(n squared), all polynomial. The power of DP comes from staying within this polynomial subproblem space: even though each subproblem might consider multiple choices (local brute force), as long as each recursive call goes to a previously defined subproblem and each subproblem is solved at most once, the total work is at most the number of subproblems times the non-recursive work per subproblem. What appears to be exponential exploration—3 to the n options in bowling—collapses to linear time because the choices for pin i do not depend on distant choices and the subproblems are reused.

ground_truth_spans.description: Explanation that subsequences are excluded because there are 2^n of them, exponentially many, so they are bad subproblem choices
DP is optimal local brute force: polynomial subproblems prevent exponential blowup even when exploring multiple options per subproblem

question: What specific items are listed on the TODAY slide visible on the board at the start of the lecture under the topic Dynamic Programming I (of 4)?
answer: The TODAY slide shown on the board lists the following items under Dynamic Programming I of 4: SRTBOT recursive algorithm paradigm, DP approximately equals recursion plus memoization, subproblems with relation via guessing and bottom-up approach, and three examples which are Fibonacci, DAG shortest paths, and bowling.

ground_truth_spans.description: Frame caption showing the TODAY slide with Dynamic Programming I (of 4) listing SRTBOT, DP=recursion+memoization, subproblems, Fibonacci, DAG shortest paths, bowling

question: What does the board show as the complete set of SRTBOT steps with their full descriptions as listed in the recursive algorithm design paradigm slide?
answer: The board shows the recursive algorithm design paradigm SRTBOT with six steps: first, Subproblem definition; second, Relate subproblem solutions recursively; third, Topological order on subproblems to guarantee acyclic subproblem call DAG; fourth, Base cases of relation; fifth, Original problem: solve via subproblems; and sixth, Time analysis.

ground_truth_spans.description: Frame caption showing the complete SRTBOT slide with all six steps: Subproblem definition, Relate subproblem solutions recursively, Topological order on subproblems to guarantee acyclic, Base cases of relation, Original problem: solve via subproblems, Time analysis

question: The lecture claims that a generic DP algorithm implicitly performs depth-first search and then connects this to DAG shortest paths. What is the specific relationship between the subproblem call graph and DFS, and how does this explain why no explicit topological sort computation is needed in the memoized DP for DAG shortest paths?
answer: In a generic memoized DP algorithm, the recursive function checks a memo table before recursing; this memo table serves exactly the role of the visited-vertex set in DFS. The algorithm thus implicitly performs a DFS through the reverse of the subproblem dependency graph—because the larger subproblem calls the smaller ones, it is searching backwards relative to the edge direction from small to large. Since the graph is acyclic, this DFS traversal naturally visits nodes in a valid topological order for the subproblem graph. For DAG shortest paths specifically, the subproblem dependencies mirror the incoming edges of G, so the DFS through the reverse subproblem graph is equivalent to the topological ordering of G, meaning no separate topological sort step is needed—it happens automatically as part of the recursive DP execution.

ground_truth_spans.description: Generic DP algorithm checks memo table as visited check in DFS through subproblem graph
DP implicitly computes topological order; for DAG shortest paths no explicit topo sort is needed because DFS does it automatically

question: The lecture explains a general time analysis formula for memoized DP algorithms and applies it to both Fibonacci and DAG shortest paths. What is the formula, and how does applying it to each of these two problems yield their respective running times?
answer: The general formula states that the total running time of a memoized DP is at most the sum over all subproblems of the non-recursive work done per subproblem, because with memoization each subproblem is solved at most once. For Fibonacci, there are n subproblems and each requires only constant non-recursive work (one addition, ignoring the large-number issue), giving O(n) time total. For DAG shortest paths, there are V subproblems (one per vertex), and for each vertex v the non-recursive work is computing the minimum over all incoming edges to v, which takes time proportional to the in-degree of v plus one. Summing over all vertices, the total non-recursive work is the sum of all in-degrees plus V, which equals V plus E, giving the familiar O(V+E) running time for DAG shortest paths.

ground_truth_spans.description: General memoization formula: total time = sum over subproblems of non-recursive work per subproblem; applied to Fibonacci gives O(n)
Applied to DAG shortest paths: sum of incoming adjacency list sizes over all vertices equals V+E total

question: The lecture distinguishes between the memoized top-down DP and the bottom-up DP for the bowling problem. What are the structural differences between these two approaches, and under what circumstances does the lecturer say bottom-up is preferable for implementation?
answer: The top-down memoized approach defines a recursive function that first checks a memo table dictionary; if the subproblem is already stored it returns it immediately, otherwise it computes via the recurrence relation, stores the result, and returns it. The bottom-up approach instead explicitly writes the base case as an assignment, then runs a for loop in topological order from i equals n down to 0, filling in each B(i) using the already-computed values for larger indices, and finally returns B(0). The lecturer states that bottom-up is a great way to implement a DP as code when the topological order can be written as a simple for loop, because it avoids all recursive calls and produces a fast single for-loop algorithm with no function-call overhead, making it very efficient in practice.

ground_truth_spans.description: Top-down memoized DP structure: check memo table, return if present, otherwise compute via relation and store
Bottom-up DP: base case assignment, for loop in topo order, relation as assignment, return original; preferable as code when topo order is a for loop

question: What is the exact running time for the Fibonacci DP when accounting for the word RAM model and the fact that Fibonacci numbers require theta(n) bits to represent, and why does this differ from the naive O(n) addition count?
answer: In the word RAM model with machine word size w bits, additions of numbers that fit in w bits take constant time. However, Fibonacci numbers grow exponentially—approximately as the golden ratio to the n—so writing down F_n requires theta(n) bits. Since n bits exceed the word size w for large n, each addition of two Fibonacci numbers near F_n takes ceiling(n over w) time rather than constant time. With n such additions needed, the total running time becomes n times ceiling(n over w), which equals n plus n squared over w. This differs from the naive O(n) addition count because it accounts for the actual cost of big-number arithmetic in the word RAM model.

ground_truth_spans.description: Fibonacci numbers require theta(n) bits because they grow exponentially; n-bit addition costs ceiling(n/w) time; total time is n + n^2/w

question: What is the specific bowling game described in this lecture, including who originally invented a variant of it and when, and what are the exact rules for how points are scored when hitting one pin versus two pins?
answer: The bowling game described is a linear bowling variant where n identical pins are arranged on a line. The lecturer attributes a related version of this game to Henry Dudeney, who invented it in 1908. The rules are: if you aim directly at a single pin i and hit only that one pin, you receive v_i points where v_i is the value labeled on that pin. If you bowl between two adjacent pins i and i plus one and knock both down simultaneously, you receive the product v_i times v_{i plus one} points. You do not have to hit all pins, and your goal is to choose which pins to hit—either singletons or adjacent pairs, with no overlapping hits—to maximize your total score.

ground_truth_spans.description: Bowling game described: n pins on a line, Henry Dudeney invented variant in 1908; hit one pin gets v_i points, hit two adjacent pins i and i+1 gets v_i times v_{i+1} product
